coredata.dc:description: The adsorption removal of lead (Pb) ions has become a crucial area of research due to the potential health hazards associated with Pb contamination. Developing cost-effective adsorbents for the removal of Pb(II) ions is significantly important. Hence, a novel fluorescent starch-based hydrogel (FSH) using starch (ST), cellulose nanofibrils (CN), and carbon dots (CD) was fabricated for simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pb(II). A comprehensive characterization of FSH, including its morphological features, chemical composition, and fluorescence characteristics, was conducted. Notably, FSH exhibited a maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of 265.9 mg/g, which was 13.0 times higher than that of pure ST. Moreover, FSH was employed as a fluorescent sensor for Pb(II) determination, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.06 μg/L. An analysis was further performed to investigate the adsorption and detection mechanisms of Pb(II) utilizing FSH. This study provides valuable insights into the production of a novel cost-effective ST-based adsorbent for the removal of Pb(II) ions.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent starch-based hydrogel with cellulose nanofibrils and carbon dots for simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pb(II)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The adsorption removal of lead (Pb) ions has become a crucial area of research due to the potential health hazards associated with Pb contamination. Developing cost-effective adsorbents for the removal of Pb(II) ions is significantly important. Hence, a novel fluorescent starch-based hydrogel (FSH) using starch (ST), cellulose nanofibrils (CN), and carbon dots (CD) was fabricated for simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pb(II). A comprehensive characterization of FSH, including its morphological features, chemical composition, and fluorescence characteristics, was conducted. Notably, FSH exhibited a maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of 265.9 mg/g, which was 13.0 times higher than that of pure ST. Moreover, FSH was employed as a fluorescent sensor for Pb(II) determination, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.06 μg/L. An analysis was further performed to investigate the adsorption and detection mechanisms of Pb(II) utilizing FSH. This study provides valuable insights into the production of a novel cost-effective ST-based adsorbent for the removal of Pb(II) ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals have been recognized as environmentally harmful due to their toxicity and resistance to degradation. Among these heavy metals, lead (Pb) ions are commonly found in natural and industrial wastewaters, often in the divalent form (Luo et al., 2014; Wang, Wang, Wang, Fu, & Zhang, 2023). Wastewater containing Pb(II) ions generated by various industries, such as electroplating, dye and paint, battery manufacturing, and glass production, necessitates effective treatment prior to discharge. The World Health Organization recommends a maximum dosage of only 0.01 mg/L for Pb(II) ions (Xie et al., 2021). To address the challenge of removing Pb(II) ions, various techniques have been developed, including adsorption (Somyanonthanakun, Ahmed, Krongtong, & Thongmee, 2023), biological treatment (Hamdan, Abd-El-Mageed, & Ghanem, 2021), chemical precipitation (Su et al., 2022), electrodialysis (Abou-Shady, Peng, Almeria, & Xu, 2012), extraction (Azra et al., 2022), ion exchange (Chen et al., 2016), membrane filtration (Soylak, Unsal, Kizil, & Aydin, 2010), oxidation (Argun, Dursun, Karatas, & Gürü, 2008), and photocatalysis (Kabra, Chaudhary, & Sawhney, 2008). Among these techniques, adsorption has gained substantial attention from researchers due to its effectiveness and cost-efficiency. Therefore, the development of novel cost-effective adsorbents represents a highly significant research area (Xiao et al., 2022).

Hydrogel is currently recognized as a highly favored adsorbent due to its high adsorption capacity, rapid kinetics, and economic feasibility. For the development of hydrogel, starch (ST) emerges as a promising choice, among numerous biopolymers, since it is abundant and cost effective (Solis-Ceballos, Roy, Golsztajn, Tavares, & Dumont, 2023). Chen et al. (2015) prepared a ST/humic acid composite hydrogel for adsorptive removal of Pb(II). Lv, Du, Zhang, Du, and Yin (2022) developed a ST-based hydrogel, demonstrating an excellent adsorption performance for Pb(II). Saberi, Alipour, and Sadeghi (2019) used ST/poly(acrylic acid) and Fe3O4 to synthesize a nanocomposite hydrogel, demonstrating a high removal efficiency toward Pb(II). These ST-based hydrogels show great potential for removing Pb(II) ions, and various methods, such as blending and crosslinking with other preferable nanomaterials, are adopted to further enhance their adsorption performance. Among these nanomaterials, cellulose nanofibrils (CN), derived from wood pulp, are abundant renewable biopolymers composed of linear glucose units linked by β-1,4 bonds (Song et al., 2022). In addition to their biodegradability and abundance, CN possess numerous desirable characteristics, including high aspect ratios, the ability to form 3D porous structures, and a high density of hydroxyl groups. Hence, the incorporation of CN holds the potential to enhance the adsorption capacity of ST-based hydrogels.

Traditional techniques for detecting Pb(II) include atomic absorption spectrometry, high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical analysis, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and so on (Song et al., 2022). However, these analytical methods suffer from various drawbacks, such as tedious operation, long response time, and high detection cost. In contrast, the fluorescent probe has garnered significant interest due to its superior properties, including simple operation, low cost, rapid detection time, and satisfactory sensitivity. Carbon dots (CD) were serendipitously discovered in 2004 during the separation and purification processes of arc-discharged synthesized carbon nanotubes. Since their discovery, CD have emerged as a highly promising fluorescent probe due to their exceptional fluorescent properties, biocompatibility, nontoxicity, and cost-effectiveness (Gokul Eswaran, Thiruppathi, & Vasimalai, 2022; Xu, Li, & Gong, 2022). Taking advantage of their fluorescent properties of CD, Pb(II) ions have been successfully detected. Sahu and Khan (2020) employed a one-pot approach with microwave treatment to synthesize boron-doped CD. Their study showcased a linear relationship between the decrease in fluorescence intensity and the concentrations of Pb(II) ions in the range of 1–10 ppb. Jiang et al. (2015) synthesized nitrogen-doped CD for the sensitive detection of Pb(II) trace levels. Their study demonstrated that the CD exhibited a linear fluorescence response to Pb(II) with a LOD of 15.0 nM. Therefore, the incorporation of CD holds the potential to enhance the functionality of ST-based hydrogels, particularly enabling them to exhibit sensing capability for Pb(II) ions.

Given the outstanding properties of CN and CD, this study aims to use these two nanomaterials as raw materials to develop a novel fluorescent starch-based hydrogel (FSH). Herein, a dual-functional FSH was fabricated for simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pb(II). Four key factors, i.e., pH, temperature, initial Pb(II) concentration, and contact time, were considered to comprehensively investigate the adsorption behavior of FSH toward Pb(II). Notably, the fabricated FSH demonstrated remarkable selectivity and sensitivity in detecting Pb(II). The absolute quantum yield (AQY) and fluorescence intensity of FSH exhibited a linear reduction upon the addition of Pb(II), with a calculated detection limit of 0.06 μg/L. Additionally, a thorough investigation into the simultaneous adsorption and detection mechanism of Pb(II) by FSH was conducted.

2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO, 98 %), sodium hypochlorite pentahydrate (NaClO·5H2O, 39 %), ethylene glycol (99.8 %), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98 %), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %), acrylic acid (AA, 99 %), N,N′-Methylenebis (MBA, 99 %), potassium persulfate (PPS, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. The ST utilized in the experiment was maize-derived amylopectin, procured from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd.

A CN suspension (1.0 wt%) was prepared through a multistep process (Guo, Liu, Hu, & Wu, 2018). Initially, 4 g of bleached wood pulp was added into 120 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, 0.16 g of sodium bromide, 24 g of sodium chlorite solution (10 wt%), and 0.03 g of TEMPO were sequentially added. A gradual addition of NaOH solution (1 mol/L) was employed to achieve a pH of 10. The TEMPO oxidation process was allowed to proceed for 6 h. The mixture then underwent a meticulous process of deionized water washing until a pH of 7 was reached. Additionally, the mixture was homogenized five times using a M110P microfluidizer. The resulting suspension underwent dialysis against deionized water for 5 days to obtain the final product. The CN were composed of cellulose, exhibiting an average length of 202.3 nm and a diameter of 5.5 nm, resulting in an aspect ratio of 36.8. Furthermore, their zeta potential value was measured at −41.9 mV. Additionally, the average carboxyl content of the CN was quantified at 0.93 mmol/g (Guo et al., 2018).

A hydrothermal method was used to synthesize CD (Boonruang et al., 2021). Initially, 25 mL of ethylene glycol was introduced into a 30 mL autoclave coated with poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon), heated at 200 °C for 5 h, and then cooled to 25 °C. Next, 13.4 mL of ammonia solution was introduced into the autoclave and subjected to another 5-h heating at 200 °C. CD was synthesized upon completing the reaction.

A 13 mL of CN suspension (1.0 wt%) was thoroughly mixed with 20 mL of deionized water by stirring. Subsequently, 0.19 g of EDC and 0.23 g of NHS were sequentially added to the mixture. Afterward, a 2.5-g CD solution (0.5 wt%) was added. The resulting solution was vigorously stirred at room temperature in darkness for 12 h. Following this, the product was dialyzed using a dialysis bag for 5 days to obtain the final product, which was designated as CN–CD.

A series of hydrogels was synthesized through free-radical polymerization by incorporating varying amounts of CN–CD (Olad, Doustdar, & Gharekhani, 2020). The procedure involved dispersing partially neutralized AA (80 %, 2.0 g) in a four-necked flask equipped with a stirring equipment, a thermometer, a condenser tube, and a nitrogen line. After 2 h of stirring, different quantities of CN–CD suspension (0.6, 1.0, 1.4, 1.8, 2.2, 2.6, 3.0, and 3.4 g) were added to the flask. Subsequently, ST solution (2.0 g, 3.2 wt%) and MBA (0.025 g) were introduced to the reaction flask, followed by the addition of PPS (0.025 g) after purging the mixture with N2 gas for 1 h to remove dissolved oxygen. The mixture was heated at 60 °C for 2 h to complete the polymerization process. The resulting hydrogels were immersed in deionized water for 4 days to remove any unreacted chemicals. The hydrogels were labeled as FSH-1 to FSH-8, depending on the added amount of CN–CD suspension. Additionally, a control hydrogel denoted as OSH-0 was prepared without CN–CD using a similar procedure for comparative analysis.

Batch sorption experiments were conducted to investigate the influence of four key factors: pH value, temperature, contact time, and initial Pb(II) concentration (Du et al., 2020). To investigate the influence of pH on the adsorption capacity, solutions at different pH levels (2, 3, 4, 5, and 6) were exposed to the FSH-6 for 48 h at 25 °C, with an initial Pb(II) concentration of 100 mg/L. To study the influence of contact time, the hydrogel was subjected to a pH 6 solution for varying durations (5, 20, 50, 100, 120, and 160 min) at 25 °C, with an initial Pb(II) concentration of 100 mg/L. Moreover, to assess the impact of the initial Pb(II) concentration, solutions with a pH of 6 and different initial Pb(II) concentrations (5, 20, 40, 80, 100, and 160 mg/L) were exposed to the hydrogel for 48 h at 25 °C. Each adsorption experiment was replicated three times.

Various analytical techniques were employed to investigate the characteristics of the prepared samples. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Phenom Prox, USA) was utilized to analyze the surface of the prepared hydrogel. The prepared hydrogel was fractured in liquid N2, and then the fractured surface was coated with gold and examined using a high voltage of 15 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher K-Alpha 1063, UK) was performed for elemental analysis. X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Bruker D8, UK) was employed to assess the crystalline structure. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were collected in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR)-FTIR mode via the iS50 FTIR Spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). For the XPS, XRD, and FTIR analyses, the freeze-dried hydrogel samples were compacted into disk-shaped forms. Additionally, fluorescence spectra were obtained through a spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000, Japan). The concentration of Pb(II) was determined through atomic absorption spectrophotometry (TAS-990, Beijing's General Instrument, China). Mechanical properties of the hydrogels were assessed using a Texture Analyzer (CT3 10 K, Brookfield Engineering Laboratories, USA). Texture profile analysis (TPA) mode with a TA10 probe was employed. The testing parameters included a pretest speed of 1.0 mm/s, a test speed of 1.0 mm/s, a posttest speed of 1.0 mm/s, and a test cycle of 2. In the context of texture profile analysis, the swollen hydrogels, having achieved swelling equilibrium at a temperature of 25 °C, were sectioned into cylindrical specimens, each with a diameter and height of 15 mm. Supporting material presents the AQY measurement.

Fig. 1 depicts the synthesis procedure employed to create FSH. In contrast to the original ST hydrogel, the FSH synthesis involved the incorporation of two main components: CN and CD. Initially, TEMPO oxidation of bleached wood pulp was performed to obtain CN. Subsequently, ethylene glycol and ammonia water were used to synthesize CD through a hydrothermal method. Afterward, EDC/NHS-assisted graft copolymerization was conducted to bind CD onto CN. CN–CD, AA, ST, and MBA were then blended thoroughly. After adding the PPS, free-radical polymerization steps were performed to produce FSH.

Fig. 2a depicts the fluorescence behavior of the prepared FSHs. It was observed that all FSHs emitted blue fluorescence upon exposure to both sunlight and ultraviolet. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity initially increased and subsequently decreased with the increment in CN–CD concentration. Particularly, FSH-6 exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity, reaching an AQY of 5.5 %. Moreover, FSH-6 has a hardness and an elasticity of 26.58 N and 0.88, respectively. Additionally, skeletal density of the prepared FSH-6 was quantified at 1.038g/cm3.

The morphological characteristics of CN, CD, CN–CD, and FSH-6, were analyzed. The SEM image (Fig. S1) revealed that CN consisted of rodlike and wirelike cellulose fibers with nanoscale dimensions. The TEM image of CD (Fig. S2) displayed a spherical shape. The CN–CD composite exhibited a rough morphology due to the deposition of CD onto the surface of CN (Fig. S3) (Qi et al., 2023). Moreover, FSH-6 exhibited a 3D porous structure (Fig. 2b). N2 adsorption–desorption experiments were conducted to determine specific surface areas of ST, CN, CN–CD, and FSH-6 (Fig. S4). CN has a specific surface area of 6.71 m2/g. CN–CD exhibited a slightly higher specific surface area than CN, suggesting that the incorporation of CD hardly affected the surface. Notably, FSH-6 demonstrated a nearly fortyfold increase in specific surface area (29.72 m2/g), which was attributed to the construction of a 3D porous structure.

XRD patterns were employed to analyze the crystallinity of ST, CN, CN–CD, and FSH-6 (Fig. 2c). The XRD pattern of ST revealed a semicrystalline structure, with a B-type crystalline peak observed at 17.0° (Warren, Gidley, & Flanagan, 2016). The XRD patterns of CN and CN–CD displayed two distinctive peaks at 14.9° and 22.6°, corresponding to the (1
1 ¯
0) and (200) crystal planes of cellulose in its crystalline form, respectively (Luo, Yuan et al., 2021). The presence of these two peaks in CN–CD confirmed that the addition of CD did not significantly alter the crystal structure of CN. In the case of FSH-6, it was observed that the intensity of XRD peaks originating from ST and CN decreased, which can be attributed to the destruction of their crystalline structure resulting from the free-radical polymerization of ST and the incorporation of CN–CD (Luo et al., 2022).

Fig. 2d shows the FTIR spectra of ST, CN, CN–CD, and FSH-6. The FTIR spectrum of ST revealed two distinctive peaks at 980 and 1165 cm−1, corresponding to the C − O − C stretching vibration (Sarmah & Karak, 2022). The characteristic CO stretching vibration band at 1053 cm−1 was detected in both CN and CN–CD cases. The functional group of –CO–NH– (1653 cm−1) was observed in CN–CD, which confirmed that CD bonded to CN via the –CO–NH– chemical linkage (Yuan et al., 2020). Significantly, the FTIR spectrum of FSH-6 showed a diminished intensity in the characteristic peaks of ST at 980 and 1165 cm−1, suggesting the occurrence of free-radical polymerization (Luo, Huang et al., 2021).

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of FSH-6 (Fig. 2e) revealed the presence of one absorption band at 280 nm, which was attributed to the π–π* transition of sp2 aromatic carbons (Cheng, Xing, & Wu, 2019). Additionally, Fig. 2e illustrates the photoluminescence (PL) excitation spectra and PL emission spectra. The optimal emission wavelength was found to be 439 nm, with the maximum excitation occurring at 355 nm. Fig. 2f presents the emission spectra of FSH-6 obtained at different excitation wavelengths. Importantly, as the excitation wavelength increased, the emission peak shifted toward longer wavelengths, suggesting the influence of surface states on the CD bandgap (Ruiz-Palomero, Benitez-Martinez, Laura Soriano, & Valcarcel, 2017). The XPS spectra (Fig. 2g) of FSH-6 revealed the presence of three distinct peaks, which were associated with carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements.

Fig. 3a–c illustrates the influences of pH, initial concentration (C 0), and temperature (T) on the equilibrium sorption capacity (Q e) for Pb(II) and depicts the relationship between these parameters and Q e.

Fig. 3d displays the sorption curve of Pb(II) onto FSH-6 at various solution pH values. The results showed that the Q e increased with higher pH values. This trend could be attributed to two factors. Firstly, the increase in pH resulted in the reduced protonation of FSH-6, enhancing the electrostatic attraction between FSH-6 and Pb(II) (Miao et al., 2022). Secondly, the increase in pH resulted in a decreased concentration of H+ ions, mitigating the negative impact on competitive adsorption and subsequently enhancing the Q e. Meanwhile, divalent Pb ions are the main form at pH ≤ 6. A pH value of 6 was chosen for subsequent experiments to focus on adsorption rather than chemical precipitation.

The change in the Q e of FSH-6 with T was observed through a series of sorption experiments conducted at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 3e, increasing T promoted adsorption, indicating an endothermic reaction (Feng, Feng, Chen, Lu, & Wang, 2022). Adsorption experiments were conducted at 25 °C for convenience.

The relationship between the Q e of FSH-6 and OSH-0 and the C 0 was examined. As shown in Fig. 3f, the Q e of both FSH-6 and OSH-0 increased with increasing C 0. Furthermore, at the same C 0, the Q e of FSH-6 was higher than that of OSH-0, indicating that the addition of CN and CD promoted Pb(II) adsorption. The Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin, and Dubinin–Radushkevich models were used to analyze the sorption data (Zhou, Luo, Liu, Chu, & Crittenden, 2017), as depicted in Figs. 3h, i, S5, and S6, respectively. Table S1 presents the fitting results. The Langmuir model showed the largest correlation coefficient (R 2). Consequently, FSH-6 showed a maximum adsorption capacity of 265.9 mg/g, which was approximately 13.0 times greater than that of the pure ST (20.43 mg/g, as shown in Fig. S7). Compared with other adsorbents used to remove Pb(II), the synthesized FSH-6 showed relatively good adsorption capacity, as evidenced by the data presented in Table 1 .

The adsorption behavior of FSH-6 and OSH-0 toward Pb(II) ions was investigated as a function of contact time. Fig. 3g illustrates the time-dependent adsorption capacities (Q t) of FSH-6 and OSH-0. Both hydrogels demonstrated an increase in Q t with contact time, with FSH-6 exhibiting a higher Q t than OSH-0. Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were used to analyze the acquired adsorption data (Zhou et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2016). As depicted in Fig. 3j and k and presented in Table S2, the results demonstrated that the pseudo-second-order kinetic model exhibited a higher R 2 compared to the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. This indicated that chemical adsorption primarily governed the adsorption process of Pb(II) on FSH-6.

The reusability of FSH-6 is crucial for its practical application (Chen, Huang, Luo, Zong, & Lou, 2021). The regeneration potential of Pb(II)-loaded FSH-6 was evaluated using NaOH solution, HCl solution, and deionized water as eluents. Among these three eluents, the NaOH solution had the least impact, while the highest desorption occurred in the HCl solution. Subsequently, an experiment was conducted to assess the adsorption and desorption of FSH-6 after the completion of Pb(II) adsorption. FSH-6 was immersed in the HCl solution for 4 h, rinsed with deionized water five times, and subsequently reused for another adsorption–desorption experiment. The results (Fig. S8) indicated that the Pb(II) adsorption capacity could still reach 83.5 % of the initial value. Hence, it was confirmed that FSH-6 possesses a good regeneration capability.

In this study, the influence of various metal ions, including Cu2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cr6+, Al3+, K+, Na+, Li+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Fe2+, and Sr2+, was investigated to assess the selectivity of FSH-6 for Pb(II). Fig. 4a clearly demonstrates a significant quenching of FSH-6 fluorescence in the presence of Pb(II) ions, while minimal changes in fluorescence were observed with other metal ions. These findings highlighted the high selectivity of FSH-6 toward Pb(II) ions. Moreover, the AQY of FSH-6 was analyzed when it was exposed to these mentioned metal ions, and no significant reduction was observed, except in the case of Pb(II) (Fig. 4b). These findings provided further evidence of the exceptional selectivity of FSH-6 for detecting Pb(II).

The sensitivity of FSH-6 to Pb(II) concentration is a crucial factor. FSH-6 was exposed to a Pb(II) concentration ranging from 1 to 2000 mg/L (Fig. 5a) to assess how FSH-6 responds to different concentrations of Pb(II). A calibration curve to correlate the AQY of FSH-6 with the Pb(II) concentration was established, resulting in a linear equation, AQY = −0.00364 × C 0 + 5.351, where C 0 denotes the Pb(II) concentration. The R 2 was calculated to be 0.9912, indicating a significant positive correlation between AQY and the Pb(II) concentration. Furthermore, the emission intensity at 439 nm demonstrated a linear relationship (R 2 = 0.9948) with Pb(II) concentration in the lower concentration range of 0.1–1.0 μg/L, and the LOD was 0.06 μg/L, with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fan et al., 2021; Li et al., 2023) (Fig. 5b). A comparison of the detection performance of FSH-6 with other previously reported fluorescent probes was conducted (Table 2 ). The results showed that FSH-6 outperformed the other probes in terms of both the LOD and linear range.

Two water samples obtained from a running water pipe and an artificial lake on the campus of Hunan Agricultural University were analyzed to evaluate the efficacy of FSH-6 in detecting Pb(II) ions. Initially, neither FSH-6 nor Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry can detect Pb(II) ions. Subsequently, a standard addition method was used alongside FSH-6 to quantify the concentration of Pb(II) ions in the obtained water samples (Wang, Zhao et al., 2023; Wang, Wang, Wang et al., 2023). The obtained results (Table S3) demonstrated recoveries ranging from 96.67 % to 102.00 %, with standard deviations below 5 %. These findings indicated that FSH-6 had potential to accurately quantify the Pb(II) concentration.

FTIR and XPS analyses were performed to elucidate the mechanism of Pb(II) adsorption using FSH-6. Fig. 6a depicts the FTIR spectra of FSH-6 before and after Pb(II) adsorption. In the case of FSH-6, a broad band at 3399 cm−1 was observed, indicating the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. Moreover, a band at 1400 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of –COO− (Liu, Li, Zhu, & Xu, 2020). After Pb(II) adsorption, both bands showed a decrease in intensity, suggesting that −OH, –NH2, and –COO− groups were effective adsorption sites.

The total XPS spectrum of FSH-6 before Pb(II) adsorption (Fig. 6b) displayed four peaks, corresponding to the C, N, Na, and O elements, respectively. Upon the introduction of Pb(II) (Fig. 6b), two new peaks emerged at 144.1 and 139.2 eV, associated with Pb4f5/2 and Pb4f7/2, respectively (Xu, Liu, & Tang, 2022). Concurrently, the peak at 496.8 eV, corresponding to Na, exhibited a decrease. The decrease in Na, coupled with the significant increase in Pb, indicated the adsorption resulting from the ion exchange between Na(I) and Pb(II). These changes observed in the total XPS spectrum of FSH-6 confirmed the occurrence of ion exchange. The O1s spectra of FSH-6 prior to Pb(II) adsorption (Fig. 6c) revealed two peaks at 531.0 eV and 532.0 eV, corresponding to C=O and –OH, respectively. Following Pb(II) adsorption, a significant movement in these two peaks indicated the presence of coordinate bonding (Jurado-López et al., 2017). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 6d) exhibited two peaks at 399.4 eV and 400.2 eV. The first N component primarily originated from neutral amine groups (−NH2), while the second N component mainly represented amide groups (–NH–C=O). A shift in the binding energy of these two component occurred after adsorption, resulting from the formation of a covalent bond between the lone pair electrons in the N atom and Pb(II). Based on these results, it can be inferred that the adsorption process included ion exchange, electrostatic interaction, and complexation.

Fluorescence lifetime decay analysis was performed to investigate the mechanism behind the fluorescence quenching of FSH-6 in the presence of Pb(II) ions. As shown in Fig. 7 , the average lifetime of FSH-6 was calculated to be 3.77 ns (red dotted curve). Upon the addition of Pb(II) ions, the average lifetime of FSH-6 decreased to 3.82 ns (blue dotted curve). The result confirmed that photoinduced electron transfer (PET) was the main mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching (Boonruang et al., 2021).

Fig. 8 shows a proposed mechanism elucidating the process of adsorption and detection of Pb(II) by FSH. Initially, Pb(II) ions were adsorbed onto the FSH surface through electrostatic attraction. The 3D porous structure of FSH facilitated the movement of Pb(II) ions from the surface to the interior by providing ion transport channels. During this movement process, numerous Pb(II) ions were attached to the 3D porous structure of FSH through ion exchange, electrostatic interaction, and complexation. Additionally, the enclosed CD within the 3D porous structure of FSH acted as a fluorescent source for detecting Pb(II) ions. The fluorescence of FSH was quenched when the Pb(II) ions interacted with CD. Overall, due to its unique structural features, FSH exhibited remarkable adsorption and detection capabilities for Pb(II) ions.

A novel adsorbent, named FSH, was synthesized using ST, CN, and CD for simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pb(II). The adsorption experiments conducted with FSH indicated that the adsorption process followed the Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models, in which FSH demonstrated a remarkable maximum adsorption capacity of 265.9 mg/g. Additionally, FSH exhibited remarkable selectivity and sensitivity for detecting Pb(II), showing linear analytical ranges of 0.1–1.0 μg/L and 1–200 mg/L. The LOD for Pb(II) using FSH was found to be 0.06 μg/L. The adsorption of Pb(II) by FSH was attributed to ion exchange, electrostatic interaction, and complexation, while the detection of Pb(II) relied on the fluorescence quenching of FSH caused by PET. Therefore, due to its affordability, effectiveness, and reusability, FSH holds a significant potential in the removal of Pb(II).

Zhixu Zhang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhengwu Huang: Software. Dan Qin: Software. Dongbo Liu: Supervision. Xin Guo: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Validation. Haiyan Lin: Supervision.
